keyIdeas: A balanced equation has equal numbers of each type of atom on both sides and equal overall charge.
To balance: write correct formulae first, then adjust coefficients only â€” never change subscripts within a formula.
An ionic equation is obtained by writing all aqueous ionic species as separate ions, then cancelling spectator ions (ions that appear unchanged on both sides).
State symbols must be included in ionic equations: (s), (l), (g), (aq).
Ionic equations highlight the actual chemical change, removing spectator ions that do not participate.
Equations for reactions studied include: metal + acid; acid + base; acid + carbonate; precipitation reactions (e.g. Baآ²âپ؛ + SOâ‚„آ²âپ» â†’ BaSOâ‚„â†“).

mustKnow.definition: An equation in which the number of atoms of each element and the total charge are equal on both sides.
An ion that is present in solution but does not take part in the chemical reaction; it appears unchanged on both sides of the ionic equation and is cancelled.
An equation showing only the species that undergo change; spectator ions are omitted; all aqueous ionic compounds are written as separate ions.

markSchemePhrases: ionic equation with no spectator ions
overall charge balanced on both sides
commandWords: 'Write' a balanced equation â€” include state symbols and check atom/charge balance. 'Write' an ionic equation â€” split all aqueous ionic compounds into ions and cancel spectators. 'Balance' a given equation â€” adjust coefficients only.
examinerTips: Check your balanced equation by tallying atoms of each element and confirming the total charge on each side is equal â€” this takes 10 seconds and avoids dropped marks.
State symbols are regularly awarded as a separate mark: (aq) for dissolved ions, (g) for gas evolved, (s) for precipitate.
For precipitation reactions, only the ions forming the precipitate remain in the ionic equation: Agâپ؛(aq) + Clâپ»(aq) â†’ AgCl(s).
When writing ionic equations for weak acids (e.g. CHâ‚ƒCOOH), do NOT split the acid into ions â€” only strong acids and soluble ionic compounds are split.
Never change subscripts to balance â€” only change coefficients.
workedExample:
Write the ionic equation for the reaction of silver nitrate solution with sodium chloride solution.

Full equation:
AgNOâ‚ƒ(aq) + NaCl(aq) â†’ AgCl(s) + NaNOâ‚ƒ(aq)

Split aqueous ionic compounds into ions:
Agâپ؛(aq) + NOâ‚ƒâپ»(aq) + Naâپ؛(aq) + Clâپ»(aq) â†’ AgCl(s) + Naâپ؛(aq) + NOâ‚ƒâپ»(aq)

Cancel spectator ions (Naâپ؛ and NOâ‚ƒâپ»):
Agâپ؛(aq) + Clâپ»(aq) â†’ AgCl(s)
commonTraps: Changing subscripts within a formula to balance an equation â€” only coefficients may be changed.
Splitting a precipitate or weak acid into ions â€” AgCl(s) stays as AgCl(s), not Agâپ؛ + Clâپ».
Omitting state symbols â€” at A-level these are always required and often mark-worthy.
Including spectator ions in the ionic equation â€” a mark is frequently given for correctly omitting them.
Forgetting to balance charge in ionic equations involving transition metals with multiple oxidation states.
